input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation is based on Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) using SysML (including v1.7 state‑machine diagrams) to capture requirements, functional, logical and physical architectures. Component‑connector structures are modeled with Architecture Description Languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc, while behavioral details may be expressed with MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines. Simulation and detailed behavior analysis are performed with MathWorks Simulink, and executable code or integration logic is developed in general‑purpose languages like C++ or Java. These heterogeneous tools and languages serve as the technical enablers for constructing, integrating, and operating the digital twin across multiple stakeholder perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin relies on Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering artifacts: structural and behavioral descriptions are captured in SysML internal block diagrams (IBDs) and the dynamic behavior is represented by a MathWorks Simulink simulation model (white‑box). In addition, data‑driven (black‑box) models such as machine‑learning predictors may be incorporated. All model‑related information and runtime observations are stored as contextual data traces that are aggregated and abstracted according to the digital‑shadow reference model UML class diagram.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The approach stresses traceability of every model and data artifact to its origin and mandates verification of model transformations (e.g., from SysML specifications to simulation models). Fidelity requirements are addressed by combining white‑box physics models with black‑box data‑driven models and by validating simulated outputs against measured sensor data. Uncertainty is recognized as stemming from model simplifications and data noise; quality assurance includes systematic verification of transformation correctness and continuous validation loops that compare digital‑twin predictions with real‑world observations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The digital shadow is defined as an automated one‑way data flow from the state of a physical object to a digital object, consisting of contextual data traces that are aggregated and abstracted for a specific purpose (Kritzinger et al.). The flow is passive; software components must interpret and process the incoming data. No bidirectional feedback is inherent to the shadow model.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: In contrast to a digital shadow, a Digital Twin can provide active components that interpret incoming data and generate command messages that are sent back to the physical system, enabling bidirectional interaction. The documents do not specify command formats, protocols, or validation mechanisms, only that the Twin is capable of pushing data to the physical object.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The digital twin lifecycle described in the documents follows the Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) development flow. It starts with the requirements abstraction, proceeds to functional architecture, then logical architecture, and finally the product/technical layer where the concrete implementation resides. At each stage the system is represented by models in languages such as SysML, ADLs (e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc) and may be refined into simulation or code artifacts. Transition between stages is managed by mapping abstract concepts to concrete realizations (concretization) and by maintaining traceability through the ‘originates‑from’ association. Governance is implicit in the need to keep the concrete model conformant to the reference digital‑shadow model.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the digital twin employs MBSE methods combined with several domain‑specific methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT). System structure and behavior are captured with SysML block‑definition and internal‑block diagrams, while component‑connector descriptions use ADLs such as ACME, AADL, or MontiArc. Behavioural detail is added with state‑machine specifications or external simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink. The development process includes a concretization step that maps reference‑model concepts to concrete classes and associations, thereby ensuring a conformance relation. Version control, testing or QA practices are not described in the provided text.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Within the Digital Twin, Processing components consume input data traces, apply engineering, simulation or data‑calculation models and produce output data traces that constitute the digital shadow. These output traces are used to generate continuous, multi‑physics‑aware insights such as the identification of joint friction levels. The results are visualised in operator dashboards, allowing human users to assess system health and trigger corrective actions. Because each output trace is linked via the ‘originates‑from’ association to its source models and input data, the confidence of the recommendation can be assessed through provenance information, enabling traceable decision‑support.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

robotics,” _Sensors_, vol. 21, no. 19, 2021.

[63] M. Liebenberg and M. Jarke, “Information systems engineering with
digital shadows: Concept and case studies,” in _Advanced Information_
_Systems Engineering_, S. Dustdar, E. Yu, C. Salinesi, D. Rieu, and
V. Pant, Eds. Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2020, pp. 70–84.

[64] P. Sapel, A. Gannouni, J. Fulterer, C. Hopmann, M. Schmitz, D. Lütticke,
A. Gützlaff, and G. Schuh, “Towards digital shadows for production
planning and control in injection molding,” _CIRP Journal of Manufac-_
_turing Science and Technology_, vol. 38, pp. 243–251, 2022.

[65] C. Idzik, D. Hilger, N. Hosters, M. Kemmerling, P. Niemietz,
L. Ortjohann, J. Sasse, A. Serafeim, J. Wang, D. Wolff, and G. Hirt,
_Decision Support for the Optimization of Continuous Processes using_
_Digital Shadows_ . Cham: Springer International Publishing, 2023, pp.
[1–21. [Online]. Available: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-98062-7_12-2)

M. van den Brand, and B. R. Haverkort, “Current trends in digital
twin development, maintenance, and operation: an interview study,”
_Software and Systems Modeling_, vol. 23, no. 5, pp. 1275–1305, Oct

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Relations to the state-of-the-art_


This work is situated in the dynamic field of digital representations, with a particular focus on purpose-driven digital


shadows. While the overarching concept of these digital representations has been a subject of extensive research, recent
efforts focus on systematic, application-specific methodologies
to address concrete industrial challenges [56], [57]. This digital
shadow approach provides a general framework for data-driven
modeling of complex physical effects, while being directly
applicable to industrial robot friction. It aligns with recent
advances in scalable identification methods, such as methods
by Sorrentino et al. [14], which avoid dedicated setups and
leverage intrinsic robot models and state data. Adaptive control
and probabilistic latent variable models further exemplify the
field’s move toward capturing nonlinear friction phenomena
more accurately than traditional static models.
A key challenge in creating effective digital shadows is the
integration of diverse modeling paradigms. Many industrial
processes, including robot dynamics, require a combination
of physics-based (white-box) models and data-driven (blackbox) models. A white-box model, such as a Simulink-based
dynamic simulation, offers clear interpretation and predictability [58]. Conversely, data-driven approaches, such as machine
learning models, can capture complex, non-linear relationships that are difficult to represent with analytical models

[58]. Current research emphasizes the need to combine these
complementary strengths to create more comprehensive and
accurate digital shadows [58], [59].
The reliability of a digital shadow is directly dependent on
the correctness of its underlying models and the transformations applied to the data. Research highlights the importance of
traceability and verification in the model-based development of
complex systems, proposing methods to ensure the correctness
of transformations (e.g., from SysML to simulation models)

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) approach described in the text. It correctly notes the use of SysML for structure, specifically citing 'SysML internal block diagrams (IBDs)' and 'MathWorks Simulink simulation model' for dynamic behavior. It also correctly captures the distinction between analytical (white-box) and data-driven (black-box) models mentioned in the abstract, and the use of the UML class diagram for the reference model. Quotes: 'structural and behavioral descriptions are captured in SysML internal block diagrams (IBDs)', 'dynamic behavior is represented by a MathWorks Simulink simulation model', 'set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstracted... UML class diagram'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly details the physical actuators and control mechanisms. The text describes the motor, gears, controller, and operational limits. Quotes: 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor... followed by a cycloidal gear', 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl', 'maximum working range to [−90°, 90°]'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text provides detailed information about the sensing infrastructure, including encoder types, placement, and data transmission. Quotes: 'There are two position encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox', 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT. These are the input current and the output angle.'

The extraction accurately summarizes the system's approach to validity through traceability and the comparison of simulated versus measured data (continuous validation). It correctly captures the combination of white-box and black-box models. While it slightly generalizes the source text regarding 'verification of model transformations,' the core concepts of traceability and validation loops are well-supported. Quotes: 'Both the models and the robot generate data that remains fully traceable to its origin', 'solution scope of the deviation of simulated data versus measured data', 'continuously improving both white- and black-box friction models during operation'.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the definition and behavior of a digital shadow described in the source. It correctly identifies the automated one-way data flow, the passive nature of the shadow, and the definition involving contextual data traces. This is supported by the source: 'A digital shadow is characterized by "an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object"' and 'digital shadow functions as a passive entity'.

The extraction correctly contrasts the Digital Twin with the digital shadow, identifying the Twin's capability for active components and the implied bidirectional interaction. The source states that a shadow 'neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system' and notes 'A Digital Twin (DT) can provide such active components'. The extraction also accurately notes the lack of specific command formats or protocols in the text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the document mentions MQTT for streaming sensor data and data linking mechanisms (e.g., Asset Administration Shells), it does not provide detailed descriptions of the broader technical network infrastructure, protocols (beyond MQTT), topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or network security. Therefore, the detailed technical connection description is effectively absent.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The text describes the implementation of digital shadows, models (SysML/Simulink), and visualization dashboards, but does not specify hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational/storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, or deployment models. Thus, the detailed hosting deployment information is missing.

_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering

is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:
